content.problemStatement:
Ada has the biggest chessboard in ChefLand. It has NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN) and MMM columns (numbered 111 through MMM), where M≥2NM \ge 2NM≥2N. As training for her upcoming world chess championship match, she placed one bishop at row rrr and column ccc of her board. The remaining squares of the board are empty. Now, she is calculating the number of squares that can be reached by the bishop in at most KKK moves. Help Ada verify her answers.

Remember that in one move, a bishop at row rrr and column ccc can move to another square at row r′r'r′ and column c′c'c′ if and only if either r+c=r′+c′r+c=r'+c'r+c=r′+c′ or r−c=r′−c′r-c=r'-c'r−c=r′−c′.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains five space-separated integers N, M, r, c and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of reachable squares.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The bishop can move to the following squares: (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 3), (2, 4), (3, 5), (1, 5)
The bishop can move to the following squares: (3, 3), (2, 2), (4, 4), (2, 4)
The bishop can only stay at the same square: (5, 5)

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to understand how a bishop moves in a chessboard. A bishop can move diagonally in any direction. We can calculate the number of reachable squares by considering all possible diagonal moves from the given position (r, c). We will use the given constraints to limit our search space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N + M)) — explain: per query is O(N + M), total is O(T * (N + M))
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — explain: we only use a constant amount of space to store the input and output values
